lessons.title: Lesson 28 — Cultural Heroes, Leaders & Legends
lessons.sub: The people and stories that made the nation
intro: The final lesson of this unit draws from Lesson 34 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum — the names of cultural figures, political roles, and legendary beings of the Haudenosaunee tradition.

Tekanawí:ta (the Peacemaker) and Aiionwátha (Hiawatha) founded the Haudenosaunee Confederacy. Their story is one of the most important political narratives in North American history — and yet most people outside the Haudenosaunee communities do not know it.
Iakoiánehr (clan mother) and Rotiiánehr (longhouse chief) are the two central political roles of the Confederacy. Shonkwaiá:tison (the Creator) closes the Thanksgiving Address.
Learning these names is learning a political and spiritual genealogy — the people and powers that made and sustain the community.
lessons.ped: Cultural heroes as curriculum: Tuck & Yang (2012) argue that decolonization involves not just learning about the present but restoring a connection to the historical and spiritual lineage. These names — the Peacemaker, Hiawatha, clan mothers — are that lineage, made speakable in the language.

exercises.prompt: Tekanawí:ta is the Peacemaker. What did the Peacemaker accomplish?
exercises.correct: United five warring nations under the Great Law of Peace, founding the Haudenosaunee Confederacy
options: United five warring nations under the Great Law of Peace, founding the Haudenosaunee Confederacy
Led a military campaign against European settlers
Established the first longhouse at Kanehsatà:ke
Wrote down the first Kanien'kéha dictionary
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tekanawí:ta — the Peacemaker. Together with Aiionwátha (Hiawatha), he brought the Great Law of Peace to the five founding nations.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Iakoiánehr — clan mother. The most important political figure in the Haudenosaunee system, who selects and can remove chiefs.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the cultural and political figures
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The founding figures and the central political roles of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Aiionwátha is often anglicised as 'Hiawatha'. What is important to note about the historical Aiionwátha?
exercises.correct: He is a real Mohawk political figure who worked alongside the Peacemaker — not the fictional character in Longfellow's poem
options: He is a real Mohawk political figure who worked alongside the Peacemaker — not the fictional character in Longfellow's poem
He and the fictional Hiawatha are the same person
Longfellow's poem accurately depicts his life
Aiionwátha is a legendary figure with no historical basis
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Longfellow's 1855 poem 'The Song of Hiawatha' is a work of fiction that appropriated the name from Haudenosaunee tradition and applied it to an Ojibwe setting. The historical Aiionwátha is a Mohawk political leader of immense importance.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Rotiiánehr (longhouse chief) serves 'at the pleasure of the clan mother'. What does this mean?
exercises.correct: Clan mothers select chiefs and can remove them — chiefs serve the people, not themselves
options: Clan mothers select chiefs and can remove them — chiefs serve the people, not themselves
Chiefs are elected by popular vote
Only men can become clan mothers or chiefs
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is the structure of the Great Law: clan mothers (Iakoiánehr) hold the ultimate authority. They select chiefs (Rotiiánehr) and can 'dehorn' them — remove their chief's antlers — if they fail the people. The system has checks and balances.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Shonkwaiá:tison — 'the one who created us'. Named last in the Thanksgiving Address, after all of creation has been acknowledged.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Shonkwaiá:tison

exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Tekanawí:ta. A name spoken with respect.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'ta'.

exercises.hint: Five syllables: shon-kwai-Á-ti-son. Stress on the third syllable. The colon (:) after á extends the vowel slightly. A weighty, complete word.

exercises.storyPrompt: Tekanawí:ta — the Peacemaker. He brought a vision of peace to five nations that had been at war. The story of how he did this — the persuasion, the ceremony, the planting of the Great Tree of Peace — is one of the great political stories in human history. Think of a moment in your own life when peace had to be actively built, not just hoped for. What did it require?

title: Unit 8 — Tools, Verb Stems & More Grammar
desc: Tool nouns, consonant-stem verbs, I-stem verbs, True E-stem verbs, and dual/plural commands

lessons.title: Lesson 29 — Tool Nouns (Famine Indefinite Class)
intro: Lesson 10 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum introduces a special noun class called 'famine indefinite nouns' — nouns beginning with the syllables ie-, ion-, iako-, teion-, tewa-. These are verb-derived nouns: the word describes what the object does.

Iekwahrahnhá:ra' (hammer) = the thing that pounds. Ionkwarihón:ni (scissors) = the thing that cuts things. Ietkarihón:ni (knife) = the cutting thing. This naming strategy — describing function — is consistent with the pattern you've seen throughout the course.
The famine indefinite class is one of the most productive noun classes for naming new objects, because any tool can be named by describing what it does.
lessons.ped: Productive naming continued: The famine indefinite class demonstrates that Kanien'kéha's productivity (Mary McDonald's term) extends to tools and technology. A hammer is not just a hammer — it is 'the thing that pounds'. Learning the pattern means you can generate novel names.

exercises.prompt: Ionkwarihón:ni means scissors. The root -hon:ni appears in several tool words. What does it likely indicate?
exercises.correct: The object that does/makes something — a functional noun suffix
options: The object that does/makes something — a functional noun suffix
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! -hon:ni is a nominalising suffix meaning roughly 'the thing that does X'. You saw it in Ietkarihón:ni (knife = cutting-thing) and Ionkwarihón:ni (scissors = cuts-things). The suffix unlocks a whole category of tool naming.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iekwahrahnhá:ra' — hammer. A famine indefinite noun describing the pounding action.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Four tools — each named by what it does.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Iakoa'sháhen (awl / hole puncher) is described as a traditional tool. What was it traditionally used for?
exercises.correct: Punching holes in hides for sewing — before metal needles, bone or antler awls were used
options: Punching holes in hides for sewing — before metal needles, bone or antler awls were used
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The awl is one of the oldest tools in the archaeological record at Haudenosaunee sites. Before European metal needles, bone and antler awls were used to pierce hides for garment construction.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Ietshennón:ni (screwdriver) is a modern tool given a Kanien'kéha name. How does this connect to what you learned about domestic animals and Ka-class nouns?
exercises.correct: All three cases show Kanien'kéha naming new objects from within its own grammatical system rather than borrowing foreign words
options: All three cases show Kanien'kéha naming new objects from within its own grammatical system rather than borrowing foreign words
Screwdrivers are an ancient Haudenosaunee tool
Ka-class and famine indefinite nouns are the same
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The consistent thread: Kanien'kéha generates new vocabulary from existing patterns. Horse = 'it carries on its back'. Screwdriver = 'the thing that turns'. Car = Kà:sere' (Ka-class). Each new object gets a name grounded in what it does.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Iekwahrahnhá:ra' — the pounding thing.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Iekwahrahnhá:ra'

exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ionkwarihón:ni — the thing that cuts things.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'ni'.

exercises.prompt: Iekwáhhere' (shovel) begins with Ie- and describes digging. How does the Ie- prefix signal the noun class?
exercises.correct: Ie- (and Ion-, Iak-, Teion-, Tewa-) are the opening syllables that mark the famine indefinite class — verb-derived functional nouns
options: Ie- (and Ion-, Iak-, Teion-, Tewa-) are the opening syllables that mark the famine indefinite class — verb-derived functional nouns
Ie- is a prefix borrowed from French
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The famine indefinite class is identified by its initial syllables: ie-, ion-, iako-, teion-, tewa-. These syllables are the surface trace of underlying verb roots — the noun is built on a verb.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.hint: Six syllables: ion-kwa-ri-HÓN-ni. Stress on the fourth syllable. The long vowel (:) before ni holds briefly.

exercises.storyPrompt: Ietkarihón:ni — knife — the cutting thing. In Haudenosaunee tradition, the knife is one of the oldest tools — used for hunting, preparing food, carving, and medicine. A language that names its tools by what they do encodes a different relationship to objects than one that gives them arbitrary names. Think about the tools in your own life: what would you call a hammer if you had to describe it rather than name it?

lessons.title: Lesson 30 — Consonant-Stem Verbs
intro: You learned A-stem verbs in Unit 3. Now meet the consonant-stem class — verbs whose root begins with a consonant (h, s, t, k). These are among the most common action verbs in Kanien'kéha.

The key difference from A-stem verbs: when the pronoun 'I' (k-) attaches to a consonant-stem root, it doubles the initial consonant. So where A-stem gives you k-á:tien (I sit), consonant-stem gives you kk-we'tarón:ko (I cut it up). The doubled consonant is the diagnostic feature.
Dualic consonant-stem verbs again use the te- prefix you know from dual pronouns and A-stem dualic verbs — the same prefix carries the same meaning across all verb classes.
lessons.ped: Cross-stem pattern recognition: Each new verb stem class reinforces the underlying grammar of Kanien'kéha. The te- dualic prefix, the -s habitual suffix, and the command-without-suffix rule all appear consistently. Learning the second stem class deepens the pattern rather than adding new rules.

exercises.prompt: kkwe'tarón:kwas means 'I cut it up (habitual)'. What is distinctive about the k- pronoun in consonant-stem verbs?
exercises.correct: The k- pronoun doubles the initial consonant of the root — giving kk- at the start
options: The k- pronoun doubles the initial consonant of the root — giving kk- at the start
Nothing — it is the same as A-stem
The k- pronoun only appears in singular forms
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! In consonant-stem verbs, k- (I) + a consonant root initial = doubled consonant. kk-we'tarón:kwas. This is the diagnostic feature that identifies the consonant-stem class.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! skwe'tarón:ko — you cut it up. Command form: s- (you) + root. No habitual suffix.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Consonant-stem verbs in four forms. Notice the doubled kk- in the first-person form.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: tetshon'ká:ria'k (you saw the board) is dualic. Why would sawing be a dualic verb?
exercises.correct: Sawing requires two motions — pushing and pulling — so it takes the te- dualic prefix
options: Sawing requires two motions — pushing and pulling — so it takes the te- dualic prefix
Dualic means two people are sawing
The te- prefix marks consonant-stem verbs
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! You've seen this logic before: tesaráhtat (run) is dualic because running requires two phases (standing → moving). Sawing: push + pull = two actions = te-. The same grammatical logic applies across verb classes.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.hint: The command form ends at skáhere' — no suffix added
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! skáhere' — you sweep. Commands never take a suffix. The word is complete.
exercises.wm: The word skáhere' is already complete — it's a command form.

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! skahó:wi — you give. A consonant-stem command used in daily exchange and ceremony.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: skahó:wi

exercises.prompt: The consonant-stem class has s- for 'you' in commands (like A-stem). But what makes the first-person form visibly different?
exercises.correct: The kk- doubled consonant at the start: kkwe'tarón:kwas vs ká:tiens
options: The kk- doubled consonant at the start: kkwe'tarón:kwas vs ká:tiens
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! A-stem first person: k-á:tiens (single k). Consonant-stem first person: kk-we'tarón:kwas (doubled kk). This doubling is the surface signal of the consonant-stem class.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: You now know two verb stem classes (A-stem and consonant-stem) out of six total. What are the six stem classes in Mary McDonald's curriculum?
exercises.correct: A stem, consonant stem, fake E stem, O/On stem, I stem, True E stem
options: A stem, consonant stem, fake E stem, O/On stem, I stem, True E stem
Past, present, future, command, habitual, dualic
Singular, dual, plural, male, female, neutral
Subjective, objective, reflexive, causative, passive, middle
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The six verb stem classes are the core organising principle of Kanien'kéha verb morphology. You've now seen A-stem (Units 3 and 10) and consonant-stem (this lesson). I-stem and True E-stem are in the next two lessons.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.hint: Five syllables: skwe'-ta-RÓN-ko. Stress on the fourth syllable. The initial skw- cluster is a quick consonant blend.

exercises.storyPrompt: skáhere' — you sweep. Sweeping is a daily household action — humble, repetitive, essential. In the Freire (1970) tradition of literacy, the most generative words are those rooted in daily life. Sweeping, cutting, giving. These are the actions that fill the hours of any life. Which everyday action in your life would you most want to be able to name in Kanien'kéha?

lessons.title: Lesson 31 — I-Stem & True E-Stem Verbs
intro: Two more verb stem classes complete your verb vocabulary toolkit. The I-stem class has verb roots beginning with 'i' — the distinctive feature is that the pronoun 'you' appears as ts- rather than s-. You also hear a distinctive (sh) sound when s falls between h and i.
The True E-stem class has roots beginning with 'é'. These are common in everyday speech — expressing putting, remembering, wanting, and mowing. The pronoun 'you' is s- as in A-stem.
Together with A-stem and consonant-stem, these bring you to four of the six verb classes. With all four, you can produce commands and habitual forms for the most common verbs in Kanien'kéha.
lessons.ped: Completing the verb system: Mary McDonald's curriculum teaches all six stem classes in Lessons 12–17. By lesson 31, you have seen A-stem (L12), consonant-stem (L13), I-stem (L16), and True E-stem (L17). The pattern is consistent: s- or ts- for 'you', k- or ki- for 'I', -s for habitual, te- for dualic.

exercises.prompt: tsí'stenht (you take scales off fish) uses ts- for 'you'. Why ts- instead of s-?
exercises.correct: I-stem verbs use ts- as the 'you' pronoun — this is the diagnostic feature of the I-stem class
options: I-stem verbs use ts- as the 'you' pronoun — this is the diagnostic feature of the I-stem class
ts- is only used with fish verbs
ts- is a dialect variant of s-
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The I-stem class is identified by ts- (you) instead of s-. When the s falls between h and i, you also get the English 'sh' sound. This is a phonological feature of the class, not a random variation.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! sé:ta — you put it in. True E-stem command: s- (you) + é:ta (E-stem root).
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Shé:kon! I-stem and E-stem verbs — four common actions.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: ské:ta' means 'do you remember?' The same root appears in ké:ta'ne's (I remember, habitual). What does this tell you about how questions and statements relate in Kanien'kéha?
exercises.correct: Questions and statements use the same verb root — the difference is in the pronoun and context, not in a special question word like English 'do'
options: Questions and statements use the same verb root — the difference is in the pronoun and context, not in a special question word like English 'do'
Questions always use a different verb
ské:ta' only means 'remember' not 'do you remember'
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! English uses auxiliary verbs ('do you remember?') to form questions. Kanien'kéha does not — the same verb form can be a command ('remember!') or a question ('do you remember?') depending on intonation and context.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: sahshá:wi means 'you want'. What is the habitual form 'I want'?
exercises.sub: Based on the pattern: s- → k-; command → habitual adds -s
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! s- (you) → k- (I), command → habitual adds -s: kahshá:wis (I want, habitually). The same pattern works across all E-stem verbs.
exercises.wm: Please try again — apply the k- pronoun and -s suffix.

exercises.hint: The command ends at sanerénhton
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! sanerénhton — you mow the lawn. Command form — no suffix.
exercises.wm: The verb is complete as sanerénhton.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'You make a fire' (command)?
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! sahsennón:ni — you make a fire. An I-stem command.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: sahsennón:ni

exercises.prompt: tenhsi'tstenhrhó:tha' means 'you are a bricklayer'. What does the -rhó:tha' suffix indicate?
exercises.correct: It is the habitual suffix marking an ongoing occupation or repeated action
options: It is the habitual suffix marking an ongoing occupation or repeated action
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The habitual suffix (-s in simple forms, -rhó:tha' in longer dualic forms) marks actions done repeatedly or professionally. 'You are a bricklayer' = you habitually lay bricks. Occupations in Kanien'kéha are expressed as habitual verbs.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.hint: Two syllables: SÉ-ta. Very short. Stress on the first syllable. The colon (:) extends the é slightly. A common, useful verb.

exercises.storyPrompt: ské:ta' — do you remember? Memory is the vessel that carries language. For many learners of Kanien'kéha, 'do you remember?' is also a question about cultural memory — of ceremonies half-forgotten, of grandparents' phrases, of a childhood word heard once. What do you remember? What are you trying not to forget?

title: Unit 9 — Fish, Birds, Numbers & Conversation
desc: Fish and bird vocabulary, numbers to 100, dual/plural commands, and everyday conversation

lessons.title: Lesson 32 — Fish of the Waters
lessons.sub: Aquatic life of the Kanehsatà:ke territory
intro: Kanien'kéha has rich vocabulary for the fish of the Great Lakes and St. Lawrence watershed. Lesson 24 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum introduces 12 species, reflecting the depth of Haudenosaunee ecological knowledge.

Several fish in this lesson carry environmental and political significance. Eel (Aterón:taks) gives its name to the Eel Clan. American eel populations in the St. Lawrence have collapsed due to hydroelectric dams — their restoration is an environmental justice issue tied directly to Haudenosaunee territory.
Sturgeon (Kahshakaié:ra') was a major food source in the St. Lawrence — the fish can live over 100 years and grow to enormous size. Lake sturgeon are now threatened.
Learning fish vocabulary is learning a map of the waters — and the threats those waters now face.
lessons.ped: Language and environmental knowledge: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023) emphasise that Indigenous languages carry ecological knowledge. Fish vocabulary encodes what species are present, which are significant, and which carry social identity (the Eel Clan). This is not just biology — it is a relational inventory of the watershed.

exercises.prompt: Aterón:taks means eel. Which clan takes its name from this fish?
exercises.correct: The Eel Clan (Rotineniotthró:non) — present at Kanehsatà:ke through the historical Onondaga resettlement
options: The Eel Clan (Rotineniotthró:non) — present at Kanehsatà:ke through the historical Onondaga resettlement
No clan uses the eel name
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Aterón:taks (eel) → Rotineniotthró:non (Eel Clan). You learned the clan names in Unit 6. The fish vocabulary and the clan vocabulary are the same words in different grammatical contexts.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Kahshakaié:ra' means sturgeon. Why is the sturgeon significant to Haudenosaunee communities today?
exercises.correct: Lake sturgeon are now threatened due to dams, pollution, and overfishing — their restoration is an environmental and territorial justice issue
options: Lake sturgeon are now threatened due to dams, pollution, and overfishing — their restoration is an environmental and territorial justice issue
Sturgeon are only used in ceremony
Sturgeon were never a food source
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The sturgeon can live over a century — some alive today were born before the Indian Act. Their near-disappearance from the St. Lawrence is part of the same colonial disruption as the near-disappearance of Kanien'kéha.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the fish to their names
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Trout, sturgeon, salmon, eel — four species of the Kanehsatà:ke watershed.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Pike, bass, sunfish — three more species of the local waters.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Namé:s — trout. Two syllables: na-MÉ-s.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Namé:s

exercises.prompt: Kotihnawá:ra's (salmon) once ran in great numbers through the St. Lawrence. What happened to Atlantic salmon runs?
exercises.correct: Dams, pollution, and overfishing dramatically reduced salmon populations in the St. Lawrence watershed
options: Dams, pollution, and overfishing dramatically reduced salmon populations in the St. Lawrence watershed
Salmon populations are at record highs
Salmon were never in the St. Lawrence
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The same colonial infrastructure (dams, industrial pollutants, commercial overfishing) that disrupted Haudenosaunee territorial rights also disrupted the fish runs that sustained their diet. Language, land, water, and food are the same system.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.hint: k
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Aterón:taks — eel. The Eel Clan fish.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'ks'.

exercises.prompt: Learning fish vocabulary, clan names, and environmental history together reflects which pedagogical principle?
exercises.correct: Language cannot be separated from cultural knowledge and relational accountability to the land (Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum, 2023)
options: Language cannot be separated from cultural knowledge and relational accountability to the land (Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum, 2023)
Vocabulary should be taught in isolated categories
Environmental history is not relevant to language learning
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! This is the heart of decolonizing language pedagogy — the fish, the clan, the watershed, and the colonial history are all present in the word Aterón:taks (eel). Learning the word honestly requires learning all of this.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.hint: Five syllables: ko-tih-na-WÁ-ra's. Stress on the fourth syllable. The glottal stop at the end (ra's) is part of the word.

exercises.storyPrompt: Aterón:taks — eel. A fish that gives a clan its name. American eels migrate thousands of kilometres from the Sargasso Sea to the St. Lawrence and its tributaries to grow, before returning to spawn and die. Hydroelectric dams now block that journey for most of them. The eel's story is about obstruction, persistence, and the long way home. What obstruction in your own life are you finding a way around?

lessons.title: Lesson 33 — Birds of the Sky
intro: The Thanksgiving Address acknowledges the birds collectively — 'the birds who use their songs to please us daily'. Lesson 25 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum introduces 12 birds of the Kanehsatà:ke territory.

The eagle (Á:kwe'ks) holds the highest ceremonial significance — eagle feathers mark achievement and carry prayers. In the founding story of the Confederacy, an eagle perches at the top of the Great Tree of Peace, watching for danger.
The goose (Káhonk) is the most visible seasonal marker in the region — their V-formations in spring and autumn are the clearest sign of seasonal change.
Bird knowledge is ecological knowledge: knowing which bird arrives when, which sings in which season, which nests where — this is the language of the land.
lessons.ped: Birds in the Thanksgiving Address: The birds are acknowledged as a collective in the Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — their songs 'please us daily'. Learning individual bird names enriches understanding of the Address: when you hear 'the birds', you now know specific birds that word includes.

exercises.prompt: Á:kwe'ks means eagle. In the founding story of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy, where does the eagle perch?
exercises.correct: At the top of the Great Tree of Peace, watching for danger
options: At the top of the Great Tree of Peace, watching for danger
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The eagle (Á:kwe'ks) perches at the top of the Great Tree of Peace (Kaienrekówa / White Pine), watching for threats to the Confederacy. Eagle feathers mark achievement and carry prayers upward.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Eagle, robin, hawk, goose — four birds of the Kanehsatà:ke territory.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Two different owl words — specific (horned owl) and general. Kanien'kéha maintains this distinction.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.sub: Think about what is distinctive about geese
exercises.correct: The sound they make — 'honk' is an onomatopoeic representation of the goose call
options: The sound they make — 'honk' is an onomatopoeic representation of the goose call
Their connection to the Turtle Clan
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Káhonk is onomatopoeic — the word imitates the sound. This is the same naming strategy as the English word 'honk'. Many bird names across languages are onomatopoeic — the sound becomes the name.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Kwáwenhs (robin) returns each spring. How does this connect to the ceremonial vocabulary you learned?
exercises.correct: The robin's return signals Sé:ske (spring) — which triggers the Strawberry Ceremony. Bird, season, and ceremony are connected
options: The robin's return signals Sé:ske (spring) — which triggers the Strawberry Ceremony. Bird, season, and ceremony are connected
The robin is associated with autumn only
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Kwáwenhs (robin) → Sé:ske (spring) → Ken' niiohontésha' (strawberries) → Strawberry Ceremony. The natural world, the language, and the ceremonial calendar are the same system.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Á:kwe'ks — eagle. Two syllables: Á-kwe'ks. The glottal stop (') is part of the word.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Á:kwe'ks

exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Káhonk — goose. Onomatopoeic — it sounds like what it names.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'nk'.

exercises.prompt: Tsistí:keri (horned owl) is associated with death or medicine in some Haudenosaunee traditions. What does learning this tell you about how Kanien'kéha vocabulary works?
exercises.correct: Words carry cultural associations — learning the word without the story gives you only half the knowledge
options: Words carry cultural associations — learning the word without the story gives you only half the knowledge
Cultural associations are not relevant to language learning
The word and the story are separate things
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is what García & Li Wei (2014) mean when they say that translanguaging involves the full linguistic and cultural repertoire. The word Tsistí:keri carries the owl story with it. Vocabulary without context is an empty container.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.hint: Two syllables: Á-kwe'ks. The long vowel on Á: is important. The final kwe'ks is a cluster — kw-glottal-ks.

exercises.storyPrompt: Á:kwe'ks — eagle. Perched at the top of the Great Tree of Peace, watching for danger. In ceremony, eagle feathers mark those who have served the community. Think of someone in your life who watches — who keeps the long view, who sees what others miss. Who is your eagle?

lessons.title: Lesson 34 — Dual & Plural Commands
lessons.sub: Directing groups — the full command paradigm
intro: You have learned the singular command form throughout the course: sá:tien (you sit), tesaráhtat (you run). Lesson 35 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum extends this to dual and plural commands.
The pattern is consistent across all verb stems: replace the singular pronoun s- with seni- (you two) or sewa- (you all). You already know seni- and sewa- from the dual and plural pronoun lessons.
This is the moment the whole pronoun system pays off: the same seni- you learned as a standalone pronoun (teseniiáhshe = you two) now attaches to verbs to tell two people to act.
lessons.ped: Paradigm completion as mastery: Nation (2001) identifies 'productive control' — being able to generate the full paradigm from knowledge of its parts — as a key marker of vocabulary mastery. Dual and plural commands complete the command paradigm you have been building since Unit 1.

exercises.prompt: seniá:tien means 'you two sit'. You know sá:tien (you [one] sit). What changed?
exercises.correct: The singular pronoun s- was replaced by the dual pronoun prefix seni-
options: The singular pronoun s- was replaced by the dual pronoun prefix seni-
The dualic te- prefix was added
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! s- (you, singular) → seni- (you two, dual). The verb root á:tien stays the same. You already know seni- from teseniiáhshe (you two, pronoun). The same prefix appears in commands.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: sewá:tien means 'you all sit'. Which pronoun root does sewa- correspond to?
exercises.sub: Think back to the plural pronoun lesson
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! sewakwé:kon (you all, pronoun) → sewa- (you all, verb prefix). The pronoun prefix is the first part of the full pronoun. The pattern is systematic across every pronoun.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The complete command paradigm for sitting: singular s-, dual seni-, plural sewa-. Same root á:tien throughout.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Singular, dual, plural — the same pattern for listening as for sitting.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: teseniaráhtat means 'you two run'. You know tesaráhtat (you [one] run). What two changes happened?
exercises.correct: s- became seni- (singular → dual) AND the te- dualic prefix remains — running is still dualic regardless of number
options: s- became seni- (singular → dual) AND the te- dualic prefix remains — running is still dualic regardless of number
The te- prefix was added for duality of people
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! tesaráhtat → teseniaráhtat. The te- prefix stays because running is inherently dualic (two phases of motion). The pronoun s- → seni- because you are now directing two people. Both changes are independent.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you tell a group of people to sit?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! sewá:tien — you all sit. The plural command.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: sewá:tien

exercises.prompt: tesewará:htat (you all run) uses sewa-. You now know the full command paradigm: singular s-, dual seni-, plural sewa-. Where have you seen these three roots before?
exercises.correct: As standalone pronouns: í:se' (you, singular), teseniiáhshe (you two), sewakwé:kon (you all)
options: As standalone pronouns: í:se' (you, singular), teseniiáhshe (you two), sewakwé:kon (you all)
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The pronoun roots you learned in Unit 1 (Lessons 1–3) are the same roots that now appear as verb command prefixes. Everything connects. Kanien'kéha grammar is a system, not a list.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the dual command 'you two sit'
exercises.hint: á
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! seniá:tien — you two sit. seni- (you two) + á:tien (root).
exercises.wm: Please try again — the root begins with á:.

exercises.hint: Four syllables: se-WÁ-ti-en. Stress on the second syllable. A clear, direct command.

exercises.meaning: You two sit / You all sit
exercises.storyPrompt: seniá:tien — you two sit. sewá:tien — you all sit. Commands directed at groups. Think of a moment when you needed to direct a group — when you were a teacher, a parent, an elder, an organiser. What is the feeling of being the one who says 'sit, listen, stand, run'? What responsibility does that grammatical position carry?

lessons.title: Lesson 35 — Numbers to 100
intro: You learned numbers 1–10 in Unit 4. Now the system extends: Kanien'kéha uses a base-10 additive system — all numbers above 10 are compounds of the numbers you already know.

The pattern is transparent: eleven = ten + one (Óia'k énska), twelve = ten + two (Óia'k tékeni). For tens: twenty = two tens (Tékeni óia'k), fifty = five tens (Wísk óia'k). One hundred is a single word: Tewá:sen.
This system is completely regular — no irregular numbers (compare English 'eleven', 'twelve', 'thirteen'). Once you know 1–10, you can construct every number to 100.
lessons.ped: Transparent number systems: Kanien'kéha's base-10 additive system is more transparent than English (which has irregular forms from 11–19). Research in mathematical linguistics (Miura et al., 1993) shows that speakers of languages with regular number systems have advantages in early arithmetic. The number system reflects the language's general productivity principle.

exercises.prompt: Óia'k énska means eleven. You know both words separately. What does Óia'k mean?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Óia'k = ten, énska = one. Ten + one = eleven. The system is completely additive.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Tékeni óia'k means twenty. Why does the order reverse — the multiplier (tékeni = two) comes first, whereas for 11–19 the ten (óia'k) comes first?
exercises.correct: For 11–19, ten is the base and the units are added: óia'k + unit. For multiples of 10, the multiplier precedes the ten: multiplier + óia'k
options: For 11–19, ten is the base and the units are added: óia'k + unit. For multiples of 10, the multiplier precedes the ten: multiplier + óia'k
Both orders mean the same thing
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! óia'k énska (10+1=11) vs tékeni óia'k (2×10=20). The position of óia'k signals the operation: final position = addition, initial position = multiplication. The grammar encodes arithmetic.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Four compound numbers — all built from the 1–10 vocabulary you already know.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Tewá:sen means one hundred. How do you say two hundred?
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Tékeni (two) + tewá:sen (hundred) = tékeni tewá:sen (two hundred). The same multiplication pattern as for tens: multiplier + base.
exercises.wm: Please try again — apply multiplier + base.

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Óia'k énska — eleven. Ten + one.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Óia'k énska

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Tékeni óia'k — twenty. Two × ten.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Tékeni óia'k

exercises.prompt: The Kanien'kéha number system is described as 'base-10 additive/multiplicative'. How does this compare to English?
exercises.correct: English has irregular forms for 11 (eleven), 12 (twelve), and 13–19 (thirteen etc.); Kanien'kéha is fully regular above 10
options: English has irregular forms for 11 (eleven), 12 (twelve), and 13–19 (thirteen etc.); Kanien'kéha is fully regular above 10
English is fully regular from 11 onwards
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Eleven and twelve do not transparently mean 'ten + one' and 'ten + two' in English. Kanien'kéha's Óia'k énska and Óia'k tékeni do. This is another example of what Mary McDonald calls the language's productive architecture.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for one hundred
exercises.hint: s
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tewá:sen — one hundred. A single uncompounded word for the base hundred.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'sen'.

exercises.prompt: Say these numbers aloud in sequence
exercises.sub: Count by tens: ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty
exercises.meaning: Twenty — try: Óia'k, Tékeni óia'k, Áhsen óia'k, Kaié:ri óia'k, Wísk óia'k
exercises.hint: Each multiple of ten is: [number 1–10] + óia'k. Counting by tens is a good way to lock in both the number words and the multiplication pattern.

exercises.storyPrompt: Tewá:sen — one hundred. A single word that names the limit of what you can count on your hands ten times. The lake sturgeon you learned about earlier can live past 100 years — Tewá:sen winters. Some of the oldest white pines in the Kanehsatà:ke territory are centuries old. What in your world has outlasted a hundred years? What would you want to outlast you?

lessons.title: Lesson 36 — Everyday Conversation
intro: All the grammar and vocabulary you have learned comes together in everyday conversation. This lesson covers the essential conversational phrases: greetings, responses, introductions, questions about time, and ceremonial closings.

You already know Shé:kon (hello) and Niawenhkó:wa (thank you very much). Now you add: how are you? (tó:kens?), I am well (Io'tenhró:ton), what is your name? (Kanié:ri iken'?), and the ceremonial closing phrase that ends speeches and sections of the Thanksgiving Address.
The negation particle Iáh you learned in Unit 3 appears here in context: Iah tó:kens (not so well). Everything connects.
lessons.ped: Functional language integration: Freire (1970) argued that literacy — and by extension language learning — must be functional in the learner's daily world. A greeting exchange, an introduction, a question about time — these are the moments when the language becomes real. This lesson is the integration point for all previous learning.

exercises.prompt: What does tó:kens mean in Shé:kon, tó:kens?
exercises.correct: How are you? / Are you well?
options: How are you? / Are you well?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! tó:kens = how are you? / are you well? Used after Shé:kon as a standard greeting pair. You know Tó:ka' (not quite/no) from the expressions lesson — the root -kens relates to condition or state.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: What is the response to tó:kens (how are you?) when you are well?
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Io'tenhró:ton — I am well. Literally 'it is going along well with me'. Compare Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne (it makes my mind happy) — both use the io'- prefix marking a state of being.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! A complete greeting sequence: hello → how are you → I am well → what is your name → thank you.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Iah tó:kens means 'not so well'. You already know Iáh from Unit 3. What is Iáh?
exercises.correct: The negation particle — Iáh + verb/adjective = negation
options: The negation particle — Iáh + verb/adjective = negation
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Iáh is the negation particle you learned in Unit 3 (Lesson 11). Iáh ká:tiens = I am not sitting. Iah tó:kens = not well. The same particle negates across contexts.
exercises.wm: Please try again — think back to the greetings and negation lesson.

exercises.prompt: Onen' ki' wáhi closes each section of the Thanksgiving Address. You now know the Thanksgiving Address vocabulary from Unit 5. What does this closing phrase do?
exercises.correct: It signals completion — 'now, that's it, truly' — before the next section of the Address begins
options: It signals completion — 'now, that's it, truly' — before the next section of the Address begins
It negates what was just said
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Onen' ki' wáhi is the hinge of the Thanksgiving Address — it closes each acknowledgement and readies the listener for the next. Learning it connects your conversational vocabulary to ceremonial practice.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I am well'?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Io'tenhró:ton — I am well.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Io'tenhró:ton

exercises.sentence: Hello, how are you? Shé:kon, tó:ke___?
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Shé:kon, tó:kens? — hello, how are you?
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'ns'.

exercises.prompt: Tsi niiohtonhátie? means 'what time is it?'. How does knowing the numbers 1–12 prepare you to answer this question?
exercises.correct: The answer uses the numbers to state the hour: Énska niiohtonhátie = it is one o'clock
options: The answer uses the numbers to state the hour: Énska niiohtonhátie = it is one o'clock
Numbers are only used for counting objects
Time is expressed differently in Kanien'kéha and numbers are not used
Only tékeni is used for telling time
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Your number vocabulary directly feeds into time expressions. Énska (1), Tékeni (2), Áhsen (3)... + niiohtonhátie (o'clock) = telling time in Kanien'kéha. Every lesson has prepared you for this.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.hint: Shé:kon (2 syllables: SHÉ-kon) pause tó:kens (3 syllables: TÓ-kens with rising intonation for the question). Natural, warm, open.

exercises.meaning: That is all / I am finished
exercises.storyPrompt: Onen' ki' wáhi — that is all, I am finished. The closing phrase of each section of the Thanksgiving Address. You have now completed nine units of this course. You have learned pronouns and people nouns, kinship and direction, objects and verbs, colours and body parts, numbers and nature, animals and birds, tools and grammar, cultural history and ceremony. Onen' ki' wáhi. Take a moment. What has this language opened in you?
